cover.title: THE MIST
cover.subtitle: The one who looks calm from the outside, until everything hidden rises at once.
cover.tagline: You seem quiet, but what you feel is deeper and stronger than people expect.

profile_scores.caption: High competitiveness and pride, low stability. You look hazy like mist, but underneath you're clear, and once things build up, they erupt all at once.

short_summary: You seem calm from the outside, but your feelings are much clearer and stronger underneath.
People often think you changed suddenly, when the truth is that something had been building for a long time.
What changes love for you is letting small feelings out before they become a flood.

full_report.headline: THE MIST
full_report.intro: From a distance, you're a calm person. Your expression barely changes, your voice isn't loud, and you don't show much of what you like or dislike. This report follows the pattern underneath that calmness: why feelings pile up beneath the surface, why they sometimes erupt all at once, and how love changes when you let small things out sooner.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: From the outside, you may seem calm.
Your expression may not change much. Your voice is not loud. You do not always show clearly what you like or dislike.
So people may read you as easy going. Even up close, part of you can remain hard to read.
But that is only the surface.
Underneath, your feelings may be much clearer.
Your pride can be strong. What you want may be clear. Once you decide something, changing course can take time.
When you care about someone, the feeling may run deeper than people can see.
But what you show can be much smaller than what you actually feel.
So people who become close to you may understand the depth only later.
They may realize there was far more beneath the calm than they expected.
chapters.profile_note: High Competitiveness · High Pride, Low Stability. You look hazy like mist, but underneath you're clear, and once things build up, they erupt all at once.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Repeat: THE SUDDEN FLOOD CURVE
body: This pattern may feel familiar.
At first, something hurts a little. You let it pass and tell yourself it may not matter.
It happens again. You let it pass again. From the outside, everything still looks calm.
But underneath, the feeling may keep building. One small moment at a time.
Then something minor can bring out a much larger reaction. You may get angry, pull away, or decide the relationship has reached its limit.
The other person may feel surprised. For you, it may feel like the end of something that had been building for a long time.
chapters.highlight: You may not have changed suddenly. Feelings that stayed hidden for too long may have finally become visible.

chart.title: THE SUDDEN FLOOD CURVE
chart.description: The pattern of hurt feelings piling up beneath a calm surface, until they erupt all at once

chapters.title: What You Got Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: You may not be indifferent. You may simply be showing much less than you feel.
items.truth_short: Feeling little and showing little are not the same thing.

items.belief: Since I don't get angry, I guess I'm okay.
items.truth: Not showing anger does not always mean there is none. A feeling can stay quiet while it keeps gathering weight.
items.truth_short: A calm surface does not always mean nothing is building underneath.

items.truth: You may want love, respect, and reassurance more clearly than people realize. The difficulty is keeping those needs inside instead of saying them.
items.truth_short: You may want more than you show.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You can be drawn to people who are calm and do not ask many questions.
Someone who hears "I'm fine" and accepts it without looking further.
Someone who does not press, check again, or ask what is underneath.
At first, that may feel comfortable because you are not ready to show much either.
The difficulty is that if you hide and they do not ask, the real problem may never enter the conversation.
The surface stays calm while only you know how much is building underneath.
chapters.highlight: Someone who accepts your calm may miss what is underneath. A caring relationship makes room for the question without forcing an answer.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need is not a greater ability to endure. You already hold a great deal inside.
What may help is letting small feelings out while they are still small.
You do not have to wait until everything feels full before saying something.
If something hurts, name it simply in the moment. It does not need to become a long explanation.
"That hurt a little." That can be enough.
Feelings are easier to work through when they do not have to carry weeks of silence with them.
chapters.action_sentence: Let one small feeling be heard before it becomes much heavier.

chapters.title: Where to Go From Here
body: You know how to hold feelings deeply.
When you care, the feeling can be strong. Once you decide, you may not change direction easily.
What you may learn next is how to let some of that feeling out before it becomes too full.
Not waiting until everything spills out at once.
Sharing small pieces as they happen, in a way that still feels safe.
That can make the relationship feel steadier and easier to understand.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time holding things inside. The next step is learning how to make small feelings visible before they become too heavy.

chapters.title: The Type You Keep Falling For

items.description: They laugh things off and move away from uncomfortable topics. At first it may feel peaceful. Later, you may realize the two of you have avoided everything important.

items.type_title: Someone Who Takes I'm Fine Literally
items.description: You say you are okay with a small smile. They accept it and move on. Without another question, the silence can feel easy until too much has built up.
They like that you seem calm and undemanding. But if they never see how deeply you feel, a stronger reaction later may seem sudden to them.

items.type_title: Someone Whose Pride Makes Repair Hard
items.description: Both of you may say, "It's fine," and wait for the other person to speak first. The distance can stay in place until the silence becomes harder than the original problem.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: The surface stayed calm enough that even you may have missed these signs.

signal_strength.label: Even when something hurts, you don't express it and just let it pass
You don't show it even when you like someone
What's piled up keeps growing, but the surface still looks calm
A small moment brings out a reaction that carries much more than that moment alone

chapters.core_signal: The longer everything looks calm, the more important it becomes to ask what has remained unsaid.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like to You

checklist.title: It's okay to say what hurts in the moment.
checklist.description: You can name a small hurt before it grows into something larger.

checklist.title: Getting angry doesn't break the relationship.
checklist.description: Expressing anger or disappointment does not automatically threaten the relationship.

checklist.title: They tell the difference between your calm and indifference.
checklist.description: They do not assume quiet always means everything is fine.

checklist.title: They're not afraid of the intensity inside you.
checklist.description: They can stay close when you show stronger feelings, without treating them as too much.

chapters.title: What Happens in Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're vulnerable to someone who looks only at the surface and assumes calm means everything is fine. If no one asks and you say very little, your own feelings can remain outside the relationship for too long.
sections.who_steadies_you: A steadier match is someone who notices changes without forcing you to explain immediately. They ask gently, listen when you speak, and do not treat your deeper feelings as a problem.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You may still feel drawn to someone who accepts the calm surface and rarely asks what lies underneath. That does not decide the outcome. What matters is whether the relationship makes room for small truths before silence turns into distance.
sections.next_signs: This time, you may notice the buildup earlier. A small hurt appears, and the familiar urge to let it pass comes with it. That is the moment to ask whether staying quiet will actually protect the relationship.
sections.try_this: When something hurts, say one simple sentence while the feeling is still small. You might say, "That hurt a little," or, "I do not want to let this build." Then keep talking over time, instead of using one response to decide what the relationship means.
sections.final_line: What matters is not one reaction. It is whether small feelings can be shared, heard, and repaired over time.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to hold feelings deeply. What's left is learning how to let small things out before they become too heavy.
action_items: This week, name one small hurt before it becomes a larger reaction.
Notice when your surface looks calm but something inside is beginning to build.
Choose people who ask gently and listen, rather than assuming quiet means fine.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to hold things inside. This report covered why your calm sometimes collapses all at once, and how you can learn to let things out safely before that happens.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
